items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.
Correct answer: 1756-1791. “1685-1750” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Mozart wrote how many numbered symphonies?
items.why: Correct answer: 41. “31” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Mozart lived 1756 to 1791. What was his age at death (in years)?
items.why: Exact answer: 35. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
